title: Unemployment drops but labour market cracks emerge
full_text:
Despite stronger-than-expected employment figures pushing the jobless rate down to 4.4 per cent, hefty revisions to last month’s data, an uptick in underemployment and falling job vacancies showed signs of a loosening labour market.
More than 40,000 jobs were created in the economy in May, 5200 of which were full-time and the rest part-time positions, according to figures from the Australian Bureau of Statistics.
While the figure came in slightly hotter than expected, with economists expecting about 35,000 jobs to be gained in the month, a downward revision of last month’s data from an initial estimate of 19,000 job losses to more than 40,000 entirely wiped out job gains in May.
The figures were welcomed by Jim Chalmers, who said the slight upside surprise demonstrated the “strength of our labour market” in the face of global uncertainty.
“It means more than 1.25 million jobs have now been created on our watch, most of them full-time,” the Treasurer said.
“This Albanese Labor government has overseen the lowest average unemployment of any Australian government in the last half a century.” The participation rate – those active in the workforce – rose to 66.7 per cent, from 66.6 per cent.
Reserve Bank governor Michele Bullock said last week that the bank was not placing too much weight on the monthly unemployment figures.
“These are monthly numbers. There is volatility. So I wouldn’t be jumping on those numbers quite so firmly,” Ms Bullock said.
The unemployment rate remains above the bank’s forecast of a 4.2 per cent unemployment rate by June this year, 4.3 per cent by December, rising to 4.6 per cent next year. The RBA said earlier this year that the non-accelerating inflation rate of unemployment, or NAIRU, would be about 4.6 per cent.
The employment-to-population ratio increased to 63.8 per cent, from 63.7 per cent, while job vacancies fell by 2.1 per cent in the three months to May 30, the first drop in vacancies since August 2025. “The labour force data is unlikely to materially sway the RBA’s assessment of the labour market in either direction, which it continues to view as ‘a little tight’. In our view, however, conditions appear broadly balanced, with signs of ongoing easing,” ANZ’s Adam Boyton said.
HSBC chief economist Paul Bloxham noted the figures were relatively backward looking, adding that the stronger-than expect result was unlikely to alter the RBA’s view on interest rates.
“For the RBA, much like yesterday’s May CPI print, it is worth keeping in mind that these figures are quite backward looking and much has happened since then,” Mr Bloxham said.
“Although there is some risk the RBA chooses to lift its cash rate further, we expect them to be forward-looking, and expect this to keep them on hold at coming meetings.” Westpac economist Ryan Wells said the bank expected the market to soften significantly into the second half of the year as the Middle East shock reverberates through the economy and interest rate rises filter through: “We are forecasting the unemployment rate to lift to a quarter-average of 5 per cent in early 2027, driven by slower hiring rather than active labour shedding, with employment growth staying slightly positive throughout.”
